questions.q: A legal RAG system retrieves chunks that contain the word 'terminate' but the retrieved text is cut off mid-sentence and unusable. The embedding model sanity checks pass. What is the most likely root cause?
options: The embedding model hasn't been fine-tuned on legal terminology
The vector store is returning results in random order rather than by similarity score
Fixed-size chunking with no overlap is splitting operative sentences across chunk boundaries
The cosine similarity threshold is set too high, filtering out relevant chunks
questions.explanation: When retrieved chunks have the right keyword but are grammatically incomplete, the chunker is splitting mid-sentence. Fixed-size chunking is the classic cause — it slices at fixed token positions regardless of sentence boundaries. The fix is to use recursive character splitting (respects sentence boundaries) or sentence-window chunking (one complete sentence per chunk).

questions.q: You're building a RAG system over a Markdown documentation site. Each page has H1/H2/H3 headers with content under each. Which chunking strategy best preserves the document structure while keeping sections retrievable?
options: Fixed-size chunks of 512 tokens with 20% overlap
Markdown-aware splitting that creates one chunk per section and includes the header in each chunk
Semantic chunking with threshold=0.8
Sentence-window with window_size=3
questions.explanation: Markdown-aware splitting creates chunks that align with the document's own structure. Including the header in each chunk ensures the embedding knows the section's topic — a chunk that just contains 'See section 3 for details' without its header has no useful signal. The other strategies ignore the document's inherent structure.

questions.q: What is the key advantage of sentence-window chunking over plain sentence chunking?
options: Sentence-window produces fewer chunks, reducing index size
The embedding is computed on the full window, giving richer signal per vector
Each sentence is indexed for precision retrieval, but the surrounding context window is returned to the LLM
Sentence-window automatically detects paragraph boundaries
questions.explanation: Sentence-window separates the indexing unit (a single sentence) from the generation unit (the sentence plus surrounding context). You get precision retrieval — the exact sentence is indexed — while the LLM receives enough context to generate a coherent answer. Plain sentence chunking would return only the single sentence to the LLM, which is often too short to interpret correctly.

questions.q: Semantic chunking embeds every sentence and splits where cosine similarity drops below a threshold. What is the primary failure mode of this approach?
options: It always produces chunks that are too small because each sentence is its own chunk
It ignores Markdown structure and splits mid-paragraph
The threshold requires domain-specific tuning; a consistent document style may produce few or no split points
It cannot handle documents longer than 512 tokens
questions.explanation: Semantic chunking relies on detecting topic transitions via similarity drops. If a document is stylistically consistent (a technical manual all in one register, or a legal contract with uniform phrasing), similarity stays high throughout and the algorithm produces very few splits or none. The threshold must be tuned per domain, and it doesn't help for single-topic documents.

questions.q: What problem does late chunking solve that standard chunking strategies cannot?
options: It reduces the number of tokens needed to represent each chunk
It allows chunks to be indexed without an embedding model
Early chunks in a document carry awareness of content from later in the document, preserving long-range context
It eliminates the need for chunk overlap by making each chunk fully self-contained
questions.explanation: Standard chunking embeds each chunk in isolation — chunk 1 knows nothing about chunks 2 through N. Late chunking embeds the full document first (getting token-level representations with full context), then extracts chunk vectors from those full-document representations. The result: a chunk early in the document has an embedding that reflects what appears later in the same document.

questions.q: You run a chunking quality check and find that 40% of your chunks are exactly at your max_chars limit, while 10% are tiny (< 20 tokens). What does this distribution suggest?
options: The recursive character splitter is working correctly on heterogeneous content
Your documents rarely contain the separators the splitter is looking for, causing it to fall through to character-level splitting
Your chunk size is too small; increase it to see a healthier distribution
This is expected behavior for sentence-window chunking
questions.explanation:
Many chunks at exactly the max size means the splitter couldn't find a separator at or before the max length, so it cut at the character level. Many tiny chunks alongside large chunks suggests the splitter found separators (like '

') within already-small paragraphs while other sections had no separators. The fix is to inspect your documents for their actual structure and adjust the separator list and max_chars accordingly.
